retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Antepartum Management and Delivery Timing:
retrieved_docs.content: While neonatal and maternal outcomes may benefit from delivery or expectant management, informed decision-making regarding benefits and risks must be discussed with the patient. Antepartum admission with close monitoring of maternal and fetal conditions may be employed with a low threshold for delivery if maternal or fetal deterioration is suspected. Findings that indicate expeditious delivery after stabilization, regardless of gestational age, can be described as fetal and maternal factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Epidemiology -- Eclampsia Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia and other hypertensive disorders of pregnancy have many of the same risk factors: chronic hypertension, younger age, those with lower education levels, and first-time mothers. [10] According to the results from a recent eclampsia study, approximately one-third of eclampsia cases occurred in women younger than 20. [9] Other risk factors include: Personal history of pregnancy hypertensive disorder Family history of preeclampsia Prepregnancy chronic hypertension Comorbid conditions such as diabetes, renal disease, and autoimmune disorders Maternal obesity (body mass index >30 kg/m 2 ) History of preterm birth less than 32 weeks of gestation [11] Low socioeconomic status Insufficient prenatal care [11] Maternal age older than 35 Multifetal gestation Interval since previous birth of 10 or more years African American ethnicity In vitro fertilization [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Initial Antepartum Care -- Function -- High-Risk Pregnancies
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancies with conditions that increase the risk of antepartum fetal demise are classified as high-risk; in these patients, increased surveillance is indicated. [39] Conditions that determine a pregnancy is high-risk are not well-defined; however, obstetrical history or maternal, fetal, and placental factors can indicate an increased risk of stillbirth, including: Fetal indications Fetal growth restriction Multiple gestation Fetal anomalies or aneuploidy Decreased fetal movement Maternal indications Diabetes (gestational or preexisting) Systemic lupus erythematous Thyroid disorders Sickle cell disease Renal or liver disease Substance abuse Hypertensive disorders Maternal age older than 35 years Obesity Obstetrical indications History of preterm delivery, fetal demise, preeclampsia Placental abruption Postterm pregnancy Placental indications Vasa previa Single umbilical cord Polyhydramnios [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia (Nursing) -- Pearls and Other issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy result in a substantial health burden, accounting fora large proportion of maternal and neonatal morbidity and mortality. Strategies to prevent preeclampsia include the identification of high-risk patients and the initiation of low-dose aspirin in early gestation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders affect as many as 10% of all pregnancies worldwide. [1] [7] These disorders account for a significant proportion of perinatal morbidity and mortality, nearly 10% of all maternal deaths in the United States. [1] Preeclampsia is one of the most serious pregnancy-specific medical conditions of increasing incidence. It remains a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality. [8] The risk of preeclampsia is increased in women with a previous history of preeclampsia (particularly if severe or before 32 weeks' gestation)and in those with antiphospholipid antibodies, pre-existing diabetes mellitus, multiple gestation, nulliparity, family history of preeclampsia (first-generation relative), elevated body mass index, maternal age greater than 40 years, and chronic hypertension or renal disease. [3] Women with a history of eclampsia are at increased risk of eclampsia (1% to 2%) and preeclampsia (22% to 35%) in subsequent pregnancies. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Initial Antepartum Care -- Function -- High-Risk Pregnancies
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal-fetal medicine specialists should be consulted in high-risk pregnancies to help determine the best management course for a patient, including the timing and mode of delivery. Additionally, antepartum fetal surveillance, ultrasounds for fetal growth assessment, and optimization of underlying comorbid conditions are some strategies used to help mitigate adverse outcome risks in these patients. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Antepartum Management and Delivery Timing:
retrieved_docs.content: It is also recommended that patients diagnosed with preeclampsia with severe features at or beyond 34 0/7 weeks gestation undergo delivery after maternal stabilization and should not be delayed to accommodate steroid administration. In cases where patients less than 34 0/7 weeks gestation are diagnosed with preeclampsia with severe features, proper stabilization of both maternal and fetal well-being should be initiated, with management usually continued in the inpatient or outpatient setting expectantly. However, there is little evidence to suggest a benefit to this practice, and this is mainly based upon expert opinion and individualized treatment plans between patient and provider. [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of preeclampsia begins with early diagnosis and intervention, focusing on adequate blood pressure control and seizure prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension In Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, including chronic hypertension with or without superimposed pre-eclampsia/eclampsia, gestational hypertension, preeclampsia with or without severe feature, Hemolysis, Elevated Liver Enzymes and Low Platelet Count (HELLP) syndrome or eclampsia present a significant risk of morbidity to both mother and fetus. Although appropriate prenatal care with close observation to detect signs of end organ damage and prompt delivery to reduce or avoid adverse effects have produced reduced morbidity and mortality, morbidity and mortality still do occur. While hypertension itself presents concerns during pregnancy, adverse effects from progression to pre-eclampsia/eclampsia along with HELLP syndrome present the primary concern. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of hypertension in pregnancy and highlights the role of an interprofessional team in evaluating and improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Eclampsia
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery Considerations Delivery timing: Eclampsia is an indication for delivery; cesarean delivery is not necessarily required. The mode of delivery should be based on various clinical features, including gestational age, fetal presentation, and cervical favorability. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Bishop Score ," for more information on cervical favorability. Due to the increased risk for labor induction failure, in pregnancies under 30 weeks gestation with an unfavorable cervix, cesarean delivery is recommended. [2] [11] Maternal resuscitation : The maternal-fetal condition should be stabilized before proceeding with delivery. Following stabilization, fetal heart rate and uterine activity typically normalize. [2] [11] Antenatal steroids : A full course of corticosteroids should be administered in pregnancies less than 34 weeks gestation for fetal lung maturation; delivery should not be delayed to complete the steroid course if the maternal-fetal condition begins to deteriorate. [2] Postpartum monitoring : In the postpartum period, seizures most commonly occur in the first 48 hours following delivery, and the seizure risk remains elevated during the first postpartum week. Due to this and the increased risk of seizure in patients with uncontrolled hypertension, clinicians should continue monitoring patients frequently during the postpartum period. [1] Clinical features of pulmonary edema should be especially looked for in patients with renal dysfunction due to the increased risk of this complication. Adjust antihypertensive treatment accordingly to prevent further complications. [2] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension In Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylaxis with 81mg aspirin is indicated for prevention initiated between 12-28 weeks and continued until delivery when 1 high-risk factor or 2 or more moderate risk factors. High-risk factors include a history of pre-eclampsia, chronic hypertension, diabetes type 1 or 2, renal disease, an autoimmune disease, especially systemic lupus erythematosus or antiphospholipid syndrome, or multifetal gestation. Moderate risk factors include nulliparity, more than 10 years pregnancy interval, BMI >30, low socioeconomic status, African American Race, family history in 1st-degree relative of preeclampsia, advanced maternal age(35+ at time of delivery), IUGR, or previous adverse pregnancy outcome. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the criteria for preeclampsia with and without severe features. Differentiate between the different hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. Assess screening tests for patients with suspected preeclampsia during the antepartum and postpartum periods. Select interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination in cases of preeclampsia to improve maternal-fetal outcomes through timely execution of care. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Antepartum Management and Delivery Timing:
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal evaluation should include ultrasonography of the amniotic fluid index, estimated fetal weight, and antenatal testing, such as non-stress tests and biophysical profiles. Fetal status may also play a major role in determining delivery versus expectant management in preeclamptic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy constitute a leading cause of maternal and perinatal mortality worldwide. Preeclampsia, with or without severe features, is a disorder of pregnancy associated with new-onset hypertension, usually with accompanying proteinuria, which occurs most often after 20 weeks of gestation and frequently near term. This disease represents a spectrum of hypertensive disease in pregnancy, beginning with gestational hypertension and progressing to develop severe features, ultimately leading to its more severe manifestations, such as eclampsia and HELLP syndrome. This disease encompasses 2% to 8% of pregnancy-related complications, more than 50,000 maternal deaths, and over 500,000 fetal deaths worldwide. Early diagnosis and prompt management are essential to preventing maternal and neonatal complications through symptomatic management and delivery planning.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Eclampsia(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past 3 decades, the outcomes of eclampsia have improved chiefly due to improvements in healthcare. However, morbidity from the disorder still occurs. At least 20% of women with eclampsia will go on to develop hypertension in subsequent pregnancies, and another 2 to 5% will develop eclampsia in a future pregnancy. Overall, multiparous females are not only likely to develop hypertension but also have higher mortality compared to primiparous women. Because there is no reliable test to predict who will develop pre-eclampsia, all high-risk women are urged to take low-dose aspirin during pregnancy. Finally, all pregnant women should be educated about the signs and symptoms of preeclampsia and when to seek medical help.

retrieved_docs.content: obstetric events. Antecedents of pre‑eclampsia and hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم) are included as maternal‑obstetric risk factors, with renal revascularization surgery (جراحة إعادة التوعي الكلوي) identified as a therapeutic intervention for hypertension related to renal arterial disease. This integrated knowledge base supports precise coding, epidemiologic surveillance, risk assessment, diagnosis, management pathways, and targeted newborn‑screening strategies for clinicians caring for pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling) and to postpartum obstetric operation. The Newborn Screening Terminology subset incorporates concepts such as pre‑eclampsia, mild hyperemesis gravidarum, and other risk factors, highlighting their relevance for neonatal screening. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications and database linkages provide an integrated framework for prenatal risk assessment, labor and delivery management, postpartum care, and newborn screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Gestation abnormality and pregnancy and puerperal disorders are both linked to the condition ولادة قبل الأوان (preterm birth), indicating that preterm birth is classified as a specific type of gestation abnormality and also falls within the broader category of pregnancy and puerperal disorders. This dual classification underscores that preterm birth represents both an abnormality of the gestational process and a disorder affecting the pregnancy‑postpartum continuum, which is important for clinicians to recognize when assessing risk factors, planning management, and providing comprehensive care to patients experiencing or at risk for early delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: these entities form an interconnected network that integrates obstetric, perinatal, neonatal screening, and renal‑surgical conditions, supporting comprehensive risk stratification, coordinated maternal‑fetal care, and ongoing management of delayed postpartum effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, serving as valuable resources for understanding and managing these entities. They are linked to مقدمات تسمم الحمل (early signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature placental separation), providing data that aid in early detection, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making for these serious pregnancy complications. In addition, databases are associated with تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (contraction of the pelvic inlet), offering information on uterine and pelvic dynamics that can influence labor progression and delivery planning. Finally, they are connected to gestation twin (twin pregnancy), supplying comprehensive records that support the monitoring of multiple fetuses, assessment of maternal‑fetal health, and coordination of specialized care. By integrating data across these conditions, medical databases enhance clinicians’ ability to deliver informed, evidence‑based obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2491
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is included within the standardized medical query “ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)”. The hypertension SMQ is classified as a member of the group of precursors to pre‑eclampsia (مقدمات تسمم الحمل). Consequently, essential hypertension, as part of the broader hypertension concept, is recognized as one of the clinical features that may precede or contribute to the development of pre‑eclampsia, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing essential hypertension in pregnant patients to reduce the risk of this obstetric complication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: tumor of the placenta (ورم خبيث في المشيمة), and the aforementioned uterine atony and sitting delivery position. These obstetric entities are also reflected in Newborn Screening Terminology, which incorporates maternal conditions such as pre‑eclampsia and mild hyperemesis gravidarum, underscoring the link between maternal health surveillance and neonatal assessment. Clinical databases are associated with all of the above concepts, providing repositories for documentation of normal and complicated deliveries, early signs of hypertensive disorders, infectious complications, multiple‑gestation outcomes, and postpartum events, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care, safety surveillance, and research within obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1744
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with several obstetric and perinatal concepts, including مقدمات تسمم الحمل (the introductory concepts of pre‑eclampsia), ال‎تِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (placental inflammation and related conditions), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia itself), and الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery). In parallel, the Newborn Screening Terminology resource classifies مقدمات تسمم الحمل, تسمم الحمل, and plod as concepts that belong to specific subsets, and it also includes الولادة بالملقط within one of its subsets. The relationship member_of indicates that ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ) (high blood pressure) is a constituent element of مقدمات تسمم الحمل, underscoring the role of hypertension in the early framework of pre‑eclampsia. Additionally, the optional procedure إنهاء الحمل - اختياري (termination of pregnancy – optional) is clinically associated with الولادة بالملقط, suggesting a link between elective pregnancy termination and the use of forceps delivery in certain clinical scenarios. Together, these interconnections highlight how databases and terminology systems map and relate key maternal‑fetal conditions, risk factors, and interventions, facilitating comprehensive understanding and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: constitute an integrated, multilingual framework for comprehensive risk assessment, diagnosis and management of normal and complicated pregnancy, labor, delivery, immediate newborn care, and related systemic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 202

Collectively, these entities form an integrated network of obstetric complications, procedural interventions, positional techniques, multiple‑gestation classifications, neonatal screening considerations, and database linkages that support comprehensive maternal‑fetal risk assessment, research, and optimal patient‑care strategies in obstetrics.
